keywords: bolt markings, fastener grades, tensile strength, bolt identification, ISO 898, ASTM

body.en: Bolt markings provide critical information about the fastener's strength, material, and performance characteristics. Using the wrong grade bolt can lead to joint failure, equipment damage, and safety hazards. Understanding markings ensures you select the correct fastener for your application, preventing costly failures and safety incidents in African construction and industrial projects.

Metric bolts use a two-number grading system (e.g., 8.8, 10.9, 12.9). The first number represents 1/100 of the nominal tensile strength in N/mm². The second number is 10 times the ratio of proof stress to tensile strength. Higher numbers indicate stronger bolts.

Look for the grade marking on the bolt head. For metric bolts, the marking will show the two numbers separated by a decimal (e.g., 8.8). The marking may also include the manufacturer's trademark. For hex boltsef="/products#bolts-nuts" class="text-primary-600 hover:text-primary-800 underline underline-offset-2">boltsef="/products#bolts-nuts" class="text-primary-600 hover:text-primary-800 underline underline-offset-2">bolts, look for the marking on the top of the head. For socket head capscrews, the grade is typically marked on the side.

Stainless steel bolts use a different marking system. Look for A2 or A4 (property class) and the steel grade (e.g., 304, 316). A2 (304) is the most common stainless steel for general use. A4 (316) offers superior corrosion resistance for coastal and marine environments. Additional markings may indicate if the bolt is austenitic (A) or martensitic (C).

When sourcing fasteners for African projects, verify markings carefully: always confirm grade matches your specifications; be wary of counterfeit bolts with incorrect markings; for structural applications, insist on third-party test certificates; and store bolts by grade to prevent mixing. African construction projects have seen failures from counterfeit fasteners—always verify markings and source from reputable suppliers.

q.en: What are Technical Guide and how are they used?

a.en: Technical Guide are essential components in construction and manufacturing. Proper selection depends on load requirements, environmental conditions, and material compatibility.

q.en: How do I choose the right fastener for my project?

a.en: Consider the material being fastened, load requirements, environmental exposure, and applicable standards. Consult with a professional if unsure about specifications.

q.en: What materials are available for these fasteners?

a.en: Common materials include carbon steel, stainless steel, alloy steel, and specialty alloys. Each offers different strength, corrosion resistance, and cost characteristics.

imageAlt: How to Read Bolt Markings - fastener solutions
metaDescription: How to read and identify bolt grade markings. Metric and imperial bolt grades, tensile strength, and selection guide for industrial applications.

faqItems.question: How do I read bolt grade markings?
faqItems.answer: Metric bolt grades: Number on head = 1/100 of tensile strength in MPa. Example: 8.8 = 800 MPa tensile, 640 MPa yield. Two numbers: First (8) = tensile/100; Second (8) = yield ratio × 10. Imperial bolts: Grade 5 = 120,000 PSI; Grade 8 = 150,000 PSI. Look for manufacturer logo + grade marking on bolt head.

faqItems.question: What do the numbers on bolt heads mean?
faqItems.answer: Metric markings: 4.6, 8.8, 10.9, 12.9 are most common. First number ÷ 100 = tensile strength in MPa. Second number ÷ 10 = yield ratio (percentage of tensile before yielding). Imperial: Grade 2 (low), Grade 5 (medium), Grade 8 (high). Always match exact grade specified - mixing grades causes structural failure.

faqItems.question: How to identify metric vs imperial bolt grades?
faqItems.answer: Metric bolts: Head shows class (8.8, 10.9, etc.) with manufacturer logo. Thread pitch is fine (1.0-1.5mm for M10). Imperial bolts: Head shows radial lines (Grade 5 = 3 lines at 120° intervals). Thread pitch is coarser (1.5-2.0mm for 3/8 inch). Measure with caliper: M10 = 10mm diameter; 3/8 inch = 9.525mm diameter.

faqItems.question: What is the tensile strength of Grade 8.8 bolts?
faqItems.answer: Grade 8.8 metric bolt: Tensile strength = 800 MPa (116,000 PSI); Yield strength = 640 MPa (92,800 PSI); Proof load = 580 MPa. This is intermediate strength - suitable for most construction and machinery applications. For critical structural work, specify Grade 10.9 (1000 MPa tensile) or Grade 12.9 (1200 MPa tensile).

faqItems.question: Why do bolt markings matter for structural applications?
faqItems.answer: Bolt markings ensure correct strength for load requirements. Using wrong grade causes: Under-specification = structural failure, collapse risk; Over-specification = cost waste, fitting problems. Critical factors: 1) Tensile strength (load capacity); 2) Yield strength (permanent deformation point); 3) Proof load (maximum before plastic deformation). Always verify markings match specification on engineering drawings.
